sentences.text: OK, this is going to take a few minutes.
When the Germans came in-- when the Russians came in-- when the Russians left, rather, I'm sorry.
When the German's came in--
That was, I think, 19-- 1939 or 1940.
I don't remember the exact-- I don't remember the exact date.
But when the war started with Russia, 1939, I think, it started.
Poland.
But then the Russians came.
I was-- I wasn't sure.
Some people tried to run away.
We didn't know where to go or what to do, so we stayed.
But it became very, very difficult.
The Ukrainians had their run.
They did whatever they wanted.
They would break in during the-- break in during the night and take stuff out.
And in the morning, the police came to investigate.
And during the night, they were the same people that we actually saw.
Beatings started.
One thing happened almost right away.
There were some Russian-- Russians lost their group to go back to Russia.
And there were about 12 or 13 people.
And one-- one of them was Jewish.
And we had a cousin living-- it was near a cemetery that she lived.
And they came on a Friday, maybe, she can give them some food.
And with that, what she had, she put in.
Around 3 o'clock in the afternoon, the police-- I forgot to tell you one thing.
To us, the Russians didn't come in.
The Hungarians came in first.
And they went to the Hungarian government, and they told them-- they-- they were working, together, with Russia.
Even Jews-- Jews were in there-- that army-- just to [INAUDIBLE].
Anyway, they came.
They took all the man into the police stations.
And they were beating them something awful.
The--
--Ukrainians.
The Ukrainians.
Even the priest was involved in this, kill the Jews.
And we decided, we are going to run to Myszkow.
And we went to Myszkow.
And in Myszkow, they found out what was happening in Bilcze, just-- And they-- they sent out, [? number ?] [? one, ?] the-- the head of-- of the Ukrainians there.
First time, they did it, they raised my mother's skirt and started beating her.
Raising the skirt hurt me more than the actual beating.
Anyways, we were-- he came, he beat us, and he-- he sent us home.
And all the way going home, we kept-- there was a mountain there.
They kept throwing stones on us.
Luckily, to us, that-- they didn't take us.
Our next-door neighbor was a policeman.
And a little bit-- he had a little bit menschlichkeit.
And he didn't let them take-- take us.
After-- one woman escaped from this prison and ran through Borshchiv, to another city.
She went to the police station, told them what happened.
And they let them go.
They were very much humane.
As I mentioned to you, that-- they even had Jewish soldiers on the front lines.
They-- they-- [INAUDIBLE], whatever it is, because they were not affected till 1944.
The Hungarians.
And then started going off lot of Hungarian people that they threw out.
Those people were actually once Polish citizens.
And since they didn't have Polish papers, out they go to Poland.
And they would drive them from town to town.
And we tried to steal them, buy out some.
And everybody kept somebody.
In-- in my mother's house, we had a woman with two broken hands that was full of vermin.
And my mother stayed with her and cleaned her and washed her.
They couldn't believe it was the same woman.
And I was the one that's distributing the people, within the Jewish people.
And that, there was a family of about 8 or 10 children.
They had a lot of children.
And wherever I brought them, they didn't want to take them.
So that they still haven't.
I went to my aunt's house, I said, Tante, I brought you the best.
[LAUGHS] And she took them Have no-- nobody else wanted to take them already.
Very Orthodox.
Oh, very Orthodox.
You could see it by the children, too.
It was like you go to Williams-- to--
Yeah, Boro Park, something like that.
Anyways, we were able to save a few people.
And the rest, they took and killed them at Babbi Yar, if you recall that.
And our troubles started, then, right after the Hungarians left.
Germans came.
They established a Judenrat.
I'm sure most of your listeners know what a Judenrat is.
And they took some people that-- a lawyer, doctor, you know, people that were in the forefront of-- how should I put it-- the Jewish elite.
And we started seeing-- we had the River Seret, it was called.
Big river.
And every day we-- the men would go and fish out dead people that were wired with-- what do you call it?
Wire-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] wire but with nails, like-- Barbed wire?
Barbed wire, I couldn't think of the word.
And there was a plenty.
They would take them out and bury them.
No, these people--
I don't know what happened.
They-- these people came, bound, down the river, with this--
Tied and that.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And--
Yes.
We pulled them out.
We pulled them out.
We were-- we were-- did-- everybody took somebody to the back of the house and buried them in the back of the house.
But other than--
No, no, no.
They-- they wouldn't-- they wouldn't let us.
We used to go at night, pull them out, sometimes.
But they covered them up and, at night, go and bury them.
And these were the good times yet.
After a while-- am I not--
After a while, there came out an edict that no Jew should have a fur, horse, wheat, anything like-- anything like this.
They took everything away.
And then we thought, this is bad.
You had both.
You brought it.
Everybody knew what everybody has, especially with live animals or with furs.
Ukrainian police collecting it for them.
No German.
No German.
The Ukrainians had-- had it over the Jewish Judenrat.
We didn't have a Judenrat yet.
Then came an edict that all the Jews have to leave town.
They were allowed to take a suitcase with him.
And they have to go to a town called Korolowka.
Who
I think all this happened in-- in-- in '41, most of it.
I didn't-- I don't remember names.
I remember, more or less-- I would say more than less-- what happened, but I don't-- my mind was not to remember dates.
And we came to Korolowka.
All Jews were-- we were able to go with a wagon, yet, to Korolowka.
Oh, I think driving to-- well, I think it was about an hour, I would say.
With a horse and buggy, maybe 45 minutes.
We just left it the way it was.
As a matter of, fact we were still figuring out, in the living room, we had a very large table and a big closet.
And the windows were small.
And I have no idea.
I keep asking, how did they take this out?
Anyways, now-- now they rebuilt it a little bit.
I'm saying they rebuilt.
I wasn't there.
Went to Korolowka, I had my mother's sister there, the one that wrote the book.
Her name was Zesta.
But we went with her to her daughter, who was married at that time already.
And they shared everything with us.
They were unbelievably nice to us.
They ate potato shells, we ate potato shells.
They-- imagine you take somebody in for two weeks, guest, and the guest stays a month, and your life is not in danger.
But you have a guest there.
And what-- how long can you keep somebody?
But she kept us.
There is-- and I'm bringing it up, because something is going to come up later.
And we were with that aunt.
With my cousin, rather, I'm sorry.
In 1941, or so, they came and--
No more school.
No more school allowed.
No more school from the day they came in.
I-- I don't remember a curfew, but they had something worse than that.
I'm getting to that part.
That-- there came out an edict that all Jews, 13 and up, have to go and register for passports.
Of course, by that time, I-- we already had the white band and the star that children from 12 years of age had to wear.
That was--
That-- it was already declared.
It was--
No.
No, this came from the Germans.
Yeah.
They kept-- not too many, because I was, most of the time, inside.
You know something I think they were SS, because they were black.
I don't even know.
Anyway, getting back to this.
For passports, because whomever they find without a passport, from that age on, they're going to kill.
My aunt was against it.
And she didn't let her children go there.
I got to tell you about-- about the hiding places, too.
We had hiding places in five places.
We had a cellar, that potatoes were in there, and we had another cave underneath it, so when we heard somebody coming, they would run in there.
We also had another one.
The best I can associate is with Anne Frank, a double-- double--
--attic.
And the floor where the horses were, under the-- what do you call those-- under the feeders, we made another hiding place.
And we had another hiding place in the straw, that you climbed in, very deep, and you stayed there.
You know, we were there a number of times.
I'm going back now to-- to when the passports.
We came to Borshchiv, you were surrounded by Ukrainian police and Jewish police.
Had to register in Borshchiv.
Also, about an hour or an hour and a half.
That--
That was the district capital.
And they took a number of people away.
Yeah, but I got out.
At that time, it was easy yet.
The rest came home.
One thing I didn't mention in the book.
Now here, in Borshchiv, they arrested about 20 people.
And they put them to jail.
And they kept them two nights.
And they let them go.
You know what they did?
They took them in to an infected room, where they had typhus.
And they came home with typhus, and half the town got sick with typhus.
I don't think I mentioned this anyplace.
And then-- So you went to register, but you really didn't register.
I didn't register.
And came back to Korolowka.
I don't know.
I have two versions of this.
But this is the simpler one, because it will take too long.
And--
This is an important question, very, very important.
My aunt put all the people in hiding place and dressed like a peasant.
And she went to Borshchiv to experiment what's going on.
And she saved a lot of people that way.
She-- she was going back and said, don't go.
Come back.
They're going to take you away.
And that's how I found out.
She warned me not to-- not to go.
And the children, she didn't even let go.
Who registered?
It wasn't so bad yet.
They put them in labor camps.
But it wasn't like a concentration camp.
It was a working camp, working in the fields and so forth.
We could bring them food.
Oh-- oh, yeah.
They were interned away already.
That's true.
But it wasn't a concentration camp, per se.
Of course, they were watched and beaten while they were doing it.
But they didn't do any killing.
And then started what they call, aktion.
They would surround the town.
Let me say something before that.
I hope it doesn't affect this.
When I came back from Borshchiv, my uncle found me a job as a-- a cowherd.
I don't know what you call that.
Takes care of--
A shepherd.
I slept in-- in-- in-- in there.
I used to take the cows into the field.
OK, now I'm going to go back.
We heard from other towns, that they're making those aktions, that people are taken.
Never knew anything about what happened-- what happened.
Anyway, so I was, meanwhile, herded away.
And one day, I started hearing terrible screaming from the-- from the city.
And I didn't know what-- what's happening.
And I was afraid to leave the cows and go back.
And Gestapo even came to the words with the Ukrainians, and they passed me by.
Did you see any Jews?
I was dressed up like a-- no.
A peasant.
And they-- they let me go.
And then at-- at night, the shooting stopped.
And I went down.
My mother and younger brother were always hiding in one place.
And I would hide in another place.
So if they catch one of us, one of us will remain.
I came home and I knew where mother should be in the-- and, thank God, they didn't take her that time.
They were in hiding [INAUDIBLE].
Floor., ?] But we walked the streets.
You saw dead bodies all-- all over.
You saw the Ukrainians carrying stuff.
Loot.
And then I spoke-- then I spoke to some-- I don't remember.
I said, yeah, it happened to me.
And not to this one.
No, this one didn't happen.
I got-- I was afraid to go back to it as peasant.
And my uncle had connection.
He got me another job as a cowherd for a little old lady, a little old lady, who was guarding that cow like she would be her-- her only possession.
And I didn't like the way her son-in-law behaved.
And I was afraid that, one day, he'll just get tired of me, and-- You know, let me tell you about the first aktion, too.
They didn't take you to concentration camps for my part of Europe.
They took you behind the cemetery.
There's already a big grave.
And I know, I keep mixing myself with two things I don't remember for sure.
Whether I was caught in the first grotto-- in the first--
--aktion or the second one.
Anyways, I'll tell-- I'll tell you that those were the-- it was told to me from the first one, so I wouldn't have to repeat myself.
They took these people, took them out of town-- out-- out of there.
They had the caves.
They had a board.
And they were sent, with a machine gun, at the entry, just-- just go.
And they fell in.
Now, I wound up there, some place.
Hm, just like [NON-ENGLISH].
It's OK
[SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I climbed.
I was one of the last ones, already.
I climbed on the board.
And they just went like this here.
And I have a scar here.
And I started crawling between the bodies.
And I crawled to where the clothes was.
Absolutely.
And I didn't know whether I'm alive or death.
There was blood all over me.
And I just climbed-- tried to get to the edge.
And I-- and they were already tired, themselves.
So I climbed out.
I'm under the clothes.
And I hear somebody coming, take-- taking the clothes.
And I climb up.
And to my luck, I see our neighbor, the policeman.
And I told him-- what did they call him-- Mikhas, that's me, your neighbor.
He says, be quiet.
I'm going to throw the clothes on top of you.
When I slow down, climb down.
He was driving a truck--
Yes.
He was-- a wagon, for the clothes.
And he really-- but he stopped.
I jumped off.
And I have to jump to-- to a glass junkyard.
And I had all my feet and my hands all-- all.
And I got, from that, a lot of boils and stuff.
Anyway, so I came home.
And again, my family was intact.
And then they decided they don't have too many people in Korolowka.
[DRINKS] So they said, every-- liked they threw us out from Bilcze, that's how we throw out to Korolowka.
And again, we-- we lived with our distant family.
The room was half this size.
It must-- Where did you go from-- Borshchiv.
From Korolowka to Borshchiv.
Not sure--
No.
You could only live where they assigned you.
But it was not a ghetto surrounding, per se.
They gave you this street and this street.
You're not allowed to go here.
You don't [INAUDIBLE] things like this.
No-- no-- no living facilities, no-- no toilets, no nothing.
As I remember, it was-- it was a horrible thing, because everybody had to go, every night, empty those buckets.
And those buckets didn't have the-- you can-- you can imagine what it-- what it was.
Nobody liked to do it, but they had to do it.
And then they-- and then the picnic started.
In Borshchiv.
They had one-- oh, yeah, before Borshchiv-- in Borshchiv, they were catching people to work.
Forced labor.
Catch-- no, they came home.
Some of them came home at night.
But there was just forced labor.
And a Jewish cop, Jewish policeman caught me.
I was with my brother.
And my brother started crying.
Please, don't take him.
I have nobody at all.
Please.
He fell to-- to his feet and started kissing his boots.
Please, don't take him.
And I don't know.
He let me go.
Anyway, this is one episode.
They made Judenrein.
Judenrein meant-- no, that was not Judenrein yet.
We was there for a while yet.
Judenrein was about a couple of months later but the same thing.
Judenrat, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were the leaders, as I said before.
And they were-- they would come to them and say, he want this many, this many people.
Another one, they start with the aktion, but you have to deliver.
Then this stopped.
And they made Judenrein.
It was very simple.
Anybody that saw a Jew has to kill them or report them.
If you do, you get everything the Jew has on them plus a reward.
And believe me, they didn't need incentives.
I was brought-- brought up the fact, give you example about a person that comes as a guest and stays for weeks and overstays his visit.
Now, my family was with a Ukrainian family.
Yeah, you credit where credit is do.
Their life was in danger.
They took you in.
And they keep you a month, two months, three months.
And the front is behind Moscow.
And if somebody comes in, you're finished.
The-- the other ones, it-- it created, too, the other-- the other thing that happened.
No.
Imagine there's three kinds of people.
Good people that don't care, they don't mix in.
Other keep-- people that keep you for a week or two, and they say, look, the front is behind Moscow.
And the biggest part were the ones that had benefits out of it.
They took away everything.
And they wanted them to go.
They don't need them anymore.
And now this is Judenrat-- Judenrein.
Like I said, not-- anybody that sees somebody that--
No.
We--
At that time, we knew already.
We-- we-- we knew that people were disappearing and nobody sees them.
Somebody ran away from one transport and told about it.
Oh, yeah, we-- we knew.
Once you-- once you fall into their hands, that's it.
The gentiles would kill you faster than the Germans.
You have a-- a sign or something?
